fact: May 11 is exactly 131 days into the year - that's like counting to 131 Mississippi's without stopping!
In the Northern Hemisphere, dandelions are typically going wild right now, shooting their fluffy seed parachutes everywhere like nature's confetti cannons.
The sun usually rises around 6 AM and sets around 7:30 PM in most of North America, giving you almost 13.5 hours of daylight.
Taurus season is in full swing until May 20, which means the sky bull is stomping around up there in the zodiac.
Tree frogs in temperate regions are often having their loudest singing contests right now - it's like American Idol but with more croaking and slime.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, leaves are turning orange and red while the north gets green - it's like the world is playing opposite day!
Migrating butterflies like painted ladies are often zooming through many regions right now, traveling up to 100 miles per day like tiny flying race cars.
The soil temperature typically hits that magic 60°F mark in many temperate places, which makes earthworms go crazy and start their wiggliest dance parties underground.
Cherry blossom petals are usually falling like pink snow in many parts of the Northern Hemisphere, creating natural carpets that crunch under your feet.
We're still 41 days away from the summer solstice, but the days are getting longer by about 2 minutes every single day - you can actually notice the difference!
Morning dew typically forms almost every day around this time because nights are still cool but days are warm, creating the perfect recipe for grass that's soaking wet at sunrise.
